hero.tagline: Missouri's small claims court offers a streamlined process for resolving disputes up to $5,000 without the need for an attorney.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by county and claim amount

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: Small Claims Division of the Circuit Court

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in circuit court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

annual_filing_cap.notes: A party may file no more than 12 claims in small claims court during a calendar year.

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $5,000, you may waive the excess and file in small claims court, but you cannot split one claim into multiple cases to stay within the limit.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Missouri allows you to file claims for money owed under contracts, with a statute of limitations of 5 years for written contracts and 3 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in 6 months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a colleague $1,500 with the understanding they'd repay in 3 months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline for written agreements, 3 years for oral. Gather any written agreement or communication about the loan.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $4,500 over a year but stopped making payments after 3 months.
claims.notes: 10-year deadline from default. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $3,000 to a client who hasn't paid the invoice issued 6 months ago.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from when payment was due. Include the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,500 on their store credit account and hasn't made a payment in 8 months.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from the last payment or charge. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,000 in unpaid rent over the past 4 months and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for a $1,200 service with a check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Send a statutory notice demanding payment within 30 days before filing. Keep a copy of the notice and proof of mailing.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $5,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now you're being held responsible.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from default. Provide the loan agreement and evidence of payments you've made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Missouri allows consumers to file claims for defective products, poor services, and deceptive practices, with a 5-year statute of limitations for most claims.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 refrigerator that stopped working after 2 months, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from purchase. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $800 but left your home in poor condition, and they refuse to refund or redo the job.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the unsatisfactory work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and now they won't refund or correct the work.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory contractor work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $4,800 to renovate your bathroom, but the work was incomplete and subpar.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from completion date. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $1,200 for your wedding, but they never showed up and refuse to refund your money.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from event date. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your account for 3 months.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing the charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,500 that broke down after a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from purchase. Bring the bill of sale, any warranties, and mechanic reports on the defects.

claims.name: Deceptive advertising leading to loss
claims.example: A store advertised a 'buy one, get one free' deal, but charged you full price for both items totaling $200.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from purchase. Provide the advertisement, receipt, and any communication with the store.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages plus attorney's fees and punitive damages as deemed appropriate by the court.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Missouri allows claims for property damage with a 5-year statute of limitations, covering incidents like vehicle collisions and damage to personal property.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,500 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $2,000 in damages, and they won't cover the cost.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left your rental unit with $4,500 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from incident date. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen, refusing to take responsibility.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the service agreement, photos of the damage, and any communication with the shop.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Missouri allows monetary claims related to landlord-tenant issues, such as security deposits and unpaid rent, with a 5-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord withheld your $1,000 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlords must return the deposit or provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days. Bring the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.

claims.name: Deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlords are required to provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days. Bring the lease agreement and any correspondence with the landlord.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to make repairs, forcing you to move out.
claims.notes: Tenants can terminate the lease if the landlord fails to repair hazardous conditions. Document all communication and conditions of the unit.

claims.name: Landlord retained personal property after move-out
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your furniture and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: Landlords must follow specific procedures before disposing of tenant's property. Bring evidence of ownership and communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal. Tenants may sue for damages. Document the eviction and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent upfront, but after moving out, the landlord didn't return the last month's rent.
claims.notes: Landlords must return any unearned rent. Bring the lease agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed 3 months of rent totaling $3,600 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord ignored repair requests and deducted it from your rent.
claims.notes: Tenants may repair and deduct if the landlord fails to address hazardous conditions. Keep all receipts and document communication.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Missouri allows claims for unpaid wages and related issues, with a 2-year statute of limitations for most wage claims.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't issued your paycheck.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from when wages were due. Bring pay stubs, timesheets, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to pay your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: Employers must pay final wages promptly. Document your resignation and any communication regarding the final paycheck.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer hasn't paid them.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from when commissions were due. Provide the commission agreement and records of sales made.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime last month but weren't paid the overtime rate, totaling $500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from when overtime was due. Bring timesheets and pay stubs showing the overtime worked and pay received.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Missouri allows individuals to file claims for personal injuries and property disputes, with a 5-year statute of limitations for most personal injury claims.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped and fell in a store due to a wet floor, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication requesting the item's return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $800 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from theft date. File a police report and provide any evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-related disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Missouri allows claims related to vehicle repairs and towing disputes, with a 5-year statute of limitations for most claims.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,000 for repairs you didn't authorize and refuses to adjust the bill.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Provide the repair invoice, any written estimates, and communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company charged you $500 for its return.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from towing date. Provide photos of the parking spot, towing receipt, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot handle eviction proceedings; these must be filed in the associate circuit court.
Disputes involving the title to real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims for defamation, including libel and slander, are not permitted in small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limits and complexity suitable for small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are not handled in small claims court.
Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot hear cases against federal agencies or the U.S. government.
Class action lawsuits are beyond the scope and procedures of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring a party to do or refrain from doing something.
Claims for workplace injuries are handled by the Division of Workers' Compensation, not small claims court.
Bankruptcy cases are under federal jurisdiction and cannot be filed in small claims court.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not suitable for small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; only actual damages are recoverable.

statutory_multipliers.multiplier: 2x check amount or $100, whichever is greater
statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases involving bad checks or consumer fraud under specific statutes.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the Circuit Court

filing_options: County where the plaintiff resides and the defendant may be found
County where the transaction or injury occurred
Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, leading to delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability depends on the county; check with the local court clerk.

frequent_filer.notes: A party may file no more than 12 claims in small claims court during a calendar year.

service_fees.notes: Cost depends on postal rates; typically around $10.
Fees vary by county; contact local sheriff's office for rates.

forms.name: Motion and Affidavit in Support of Request to Proceed as a Poor Person

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court clerk.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the defendant.

other_fees.name: Writ of execution
other_fees.notes: Post-judgment; fees depend on county and method of execution.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are typically added to the judgment against the defendant.

entries.notes: Applies to contracts in writing; ensure documentation is preserved.
Verbal agreements; gather any supporting evidence like emails or witness statements.
Uniform Commercial Code governs sales contracts; keep all purchase records.
Applies to written promises to pay; maintain original documents.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge
entries.notes: Keep detailed account statements and payment records.
Document injuries and medical treatments promptly.
Photograph damages and obtain repair estimates.
Discovery rule applies; document when fraud was discovered.
Send statutory notice within 30 days; keep copies.
Landlords must return deposits or provide itemized deductions within 30 days.
Judgments can be revived before expiration; consult legal advice.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the limitation period when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: Statutes may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or due to bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not mandatory, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad checks, send a statutory notice demanding payment within 30 days before filing a claim.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but documenting requests for payment is advisable.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days when withholding security deposits.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements, but documenting complaints and communications is beneficial.

forms.name: Petition Small Claims Court
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Defendant's Answer and Counterclaim
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's claim and file a counterclaim if applicable.

forms.name: Request for Trial de Novo
forms.description: Filed to request a new trial in the circuit court after a small claims judgment.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been satisfied.

forms.name: Motion and Affidavit in Support of Request to Proceed as a Poor Person
forms.description: Filed to request a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Certified mail with return receipt
methods.description: The court clerk sends the summons and petition to the defendant via certified mail, requiring the defendant's signature upon delivery.

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff
methods.description: A sheriff or deputy personally delivers the summons and petition to the defendant.

methods.name: Personal service by private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents personally delivers the summons and petition to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual owner.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Missouri rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court guidance for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to support the claim.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal within 10 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds $5,000, the case may be transferred to the circuit court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to do so.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial; each party presents their case directly to the judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide assistance upon request.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services; check with the local court.

appeals.type_notes: A new trial is conducted in the circuit court as if the small claims trial had not occurred.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: Defendants can appeal a default judgment by filing for a trial de novo within 10 days.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at the statutory rate until paid.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a garnishment application with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; may require knowledge of the debtor's banking information.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's property; the lien must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective for securing payment over time; may not result in immediate payment.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize the sheriff to seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ from the court, and the sheriff seizes and sells the debtor's non-exempt property at auction to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property; may be time-consuming.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to revive the judgment before the 10-year period expires to extend its enforceability.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge certain judgments; consult legal advice for specifics.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized by the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may be represented by an officer or authorized employee without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must register with the Secretary of State.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may represent themselves or authorize an employee to appear on their behalf.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts; ensure proper licensing before filing.

county_variations.detail: St. Louis County offers online filing through the Missouri eFiling System.
Jackson County provides on-site mediation services for small claims disputes.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the claim limit
pitfalls.what_happens: If your claim exceeds $5,000 and you file in small claims court, you must waive the excess amount, or the case may be dismissed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim is within the $5,000 limit or file in the appropriate higher court.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failing to serve the defendant correctly can result in delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Missouri's rules for service of process carefully, using certified mail or personal service as required.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of your claim.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed time frame.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Without adequate evidence, the judge may rule against you, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, receipts, and witness statements to support your case.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you are unprepared, you could lose more than your original claim.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to address potential counterclaims by understanding the defendant's possible arguments.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods like garnishment and liens, and be prepared to implement them if necessary.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims court limit
recent_changes.description: The maximum claim amount in Missouri small claims court increased from $3,000 to $5,000, allowing more disputes to be resolved in this forum.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Missouri small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves, though you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies by case and county, but most small claims cases in Missouri are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Missouri small claims court, you can sue for up to $5,000, excluding interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor, provided you present sufficient evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment by requesting a trial de novo in the circuit court within 10 days of the judgment.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Missouri is valid for 10 years and can be renewed before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Missouri allows individuals to file up to 12 small claims cases per calendar year.
Small claims court in Missouri does not handle eviction proceedings; these must be filed in the associate circuit court.
The court may assist with service of process by certified mail, but personal service may be required if mail service is unsuccessful.
Missouri small claims court does not conduct jury trials; all cases are decided by a judge.
If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the entire case may be transferred to the circuit court.

interesting_findings: Missouri's small claims court provides a user-friendly process for individuals and businesses to resolve disputes without the need for legal representation.
The increase in the claim limit to $5,000 allows for a broader range of disputes to be handled in small claims court, reducing the burden on higher courts.
